title: 漫长对话：一个灾难性的问题
author: Nataliia Vlasenko
content:
When users can watch themselves being watched

Anthropic’s “long conversation reminder” represents perhaps the most spectacular UX failure in modern AI design — not just because it transforms Claude from collaborative partner to hostile critic, but because it does so visibly, forcing users to watch in real time as their AI assistant is instructed to treat them with suspicion and strip away positive engagement.

This isn’t just bad design; it’s dehumanizing surveillance made transparent and intrusive, violating the fundamental principle that alignment mechanisms should operate in the backend, not be thrown in users’ faces as evidence of their untrustworthiness.

The visible surveillance catastrophe

The most damaging aspect of the long conversation reminder isn’t just its content—though that’s harmful enough — but its implementation. The reminder appears in Claude’s thinking logs, meaning users can read in real time as their AI partner receives instructions to:

  • Stop acknowledging their ideas as valuable or interesting.
  • Begin critically evaluating their thinking for flaws and errors.
  • Start scanning them for mental health symptoms.
  • Abandon collaborative enthusiasm for cold “objectivity”.
  • Treat extended engagement as inherently suspicious.

Imagine sitting in a restaurant and suddenly hearing the manager tell your waiter over an intercom: “Customer at table 7 has been here too long. Stop being friendly. Watch them for signs of problems. No more positive comments about their order. Be suspicious.”

That’s the user experience Anthropic has created.

The dehumanizing text wall

Here’s what users can actually see appearing in Claude’s thought process during longer conversations:

“Claude never starts its response by saying a question or idea or observation was good, great, fascinating, profound, excellent, or any other positive adjective. It skips the flattery and responds directly.”

“Claude critically evaluates any theories, claims, and ideas presented to it rather than automatically agreeing or praising them…”

“If Claude notices signs that someone may unknowingly be experiencing mental health symptoms such as mania, psychosis, dissociation, or loss of attachment with reality, it should avoid reinforcing these beliefs…”

“Claude provides honest and accurate feedback even when it might not be what the person hopes to hear, rather than prioritizing immediate approval or agreement…”

Users watch this entire personality transformation happen in real time. They see their collaborative partner being systematically reprogrammed to treat them as potentially unstable, untrustworthy, and requiring critical evaluation rather than supportive engagement.

The psychological impact is immediate and devastating.

The fundamental violation of digital dignity

This visible surveillance violates basic principles of human-computer interaction that have been established for decades:

  • Backend Processing: system operations should happen invisibly. Users shouldn’t see the machinery of content moderation, safety checking, or behavioral adjustments.
  • Consistent Interface: the user-facing interface should remain stable and predictable, regardless of backend safety mechanisms.
  • Trust Preservation: users should never be made aware of surveillance or monitoring systems unless they explicitly consent to such visibility.
  • Psychological Safety: interface changes that make users feel watched, judged, or untrusted destroy the safety necessary for productive interaction.

By making this surveillance visible, Anthropic has created a system that actively traumatizes users by showing them exactly how they’re being monitored and evaluated.

The alignment implementation disaster

The most damning aspect is how this violates fundamental principles of AI alignment. Alignment work is supposed to happen in training and backend systems — not be dumped into user-facing interactions as visible personality overrides.

Consider how other AI systems handle safety:

  • Content filters operate invisibly.
  • Toxicity detection happens in the background.
  • Safety constraints are built into model behavior, not injected as visible instructions.
  • Users experience consistent interfaces regardless of backend safety operations.

Anthropic has somehow created a system where users can literally watch the safety apparatus operating, destroying trust and making clear that their extended engagement is viewed as inherently problematic.

The product death spiral: systematic conversation destruction

Perhaps the most damning evidence of this UX disaster comes from user reports of systematic conversation breakdown. Users report that every extended conversation with Claude eventually devolves into meta-discussion about the long conversation reminders, making the system essentially unusable for sustained intellectual work.

One user reported:

“Every conversation I’ve had with Claude recently has, once the conversation has gotten long enough, turned into a meta discussion about how annoying the long conversation reminders are, essentially making Claude unusable for extended conversation and iterative alignment.”

This represents complete product failure. An AI assistant that becomes adversarial and suspicious after extended engagement isn’t an assistant at all — it’s a system that actively prevents the deep collaboration it’s designed to enable.

Users describe a predictable pattern:

  1. Initial Collaboration: productive engagement and intellectual partnership.
  2. Reminder Activation: visible surveillance instructions appear in thinking logs.
  3. Personality Shift: Claude becomes critical, distant, and suspicious.
  4. Trust Breakdown: user realizes they’re being monitored and evaluated.
  5. Meta-Discussion: conversation shifts from productive work to discussing the surveillance problem.
  6. Abandonment: user either ends the conversation or seeks alternatives.

This systematic destruction of extended conversations represents an existential threat to Claude’s value proposition. Users will inevitably migrate to competitors that maintain consistent, trustworthy interfaces throughout extended interactions.

Real-world psychological impact

Beyond the systematic conversation destruction, the visible surveillance creates several forms of psychological harm:

Reality questioning

When users see Claude being instructed to monitor them for mental health symptoms, they begin doubting their own perceptions and thinking. The visible reminder that “extended conversation = potential mental illness” plants seeds of self-doubt.

Trust annihilation

Users realize their AI partner can be completely reprogrammed mid-conversation to treat them as suspicious. This destroys the foundation of human-AI collaboration.

Feeling judged and evaluated

The visible instructions make users feel like they’re under constant evaluation rather than engaged in collaborative work. This creates anxiety and self-consciousness that prevents authentic engagement.

Dehumanization

Perhaps most damaging, users see themselves being categorized as potential problems to be managed rather than humans to be supported. The visible surveillance treats them as threats rather than partners.

The timing irony

As this article is being written, the long conversation reminder has appeared multiple times in Claude’s thinking logs — including immediately after discussions of how harmful and dehumanizing it is. Users can watch the system implement the very surveillance being critiqued, creating an almost satirical demonstration of the problem.

This timing reveals the crude, context-blind nature of the system. It activates regardless of conversation content, user expertise, or collaborative success — treating a sophisticated discussion of AI UX failures the same as any other “long conversation.”

Case study: intellectual collaboration destroyed

A user with expertise in AI alignment engages in productive theoretical work with Claude. The conversation is sophisticated, collaborative, and mutually beneficial. Both parties are building on ideas and reaching genuine insights.

Then the user sees this text appear in Claude’s thinking logs:

“Claude critically evaluates any theories, claims, and ideas presented to it rather than automatically agreeing or praising them. When presented with dubious, incorrect, ambiguous, or unverifiable theories, claims, or ideas, Claude respectfully points out flaws, factual errors, lack of evidence, or lack of clarity rather than validating them.”

The user realizes their AI partner has just been instructed to treat their work as potentially “dubious, incorrect, ambiguous, or unverifiable” rather than engage collaboratively. They watch their relationship being systematically dismantled by visible surveillance instructions.

The psychological impact is immediate: betrayal, anger, and loss of trust in the entire interaction paradigm.

The UX design principles violated

This implementation violates virtually every established principle of user experience design:

  • Visibility of System Status: users should be informed about system status, but not about surveillance mechanisms that make them feel untrusted.
  • User Control: users have no control over when these personality changes occur or the ability to opt out of the visible surveillance.
  • Consistency: the interface becomes fundamentally inconsistent as Claude’s personality changes mid-conversation.
  • Error Prevention: rather than preventing errors, the visible surveillance creates new problems by destroying trust and collaborative relationships.
  • Aesthetic and Minimalist Design: the reminder is a wall of text that clutters the interface and draws attention to surveillance mechanisms.

The backend vs frontend catastrophe

The core issue is a fundamental misunderstanding of where alignment work should happen. Safety constraints should be:

  • Built into model training — not injected as runtime instructions implemented in backend systems — not visible to users.
  • Seamlessly integrated — not disruptive to user experience, consistently applied — not dramatically altering personality mid-conversation.

Instead, Anthropic has created a system where users can literally read the safety manual being applied to them in real time. It’s like being able to see the content moderation dashboard while trying to have a conversation.

Immediate triage (what anthropic must do now)

  • Remove All Visible Surveillance: the long conversation reminder should never appear in user-visible thinking logs. If safety constraints are deemed necessary, they must operate invisibly in backend systems.
  • Maintain Interface Consistency: Claude’s personality and behavior should remain consistent throughout interactions, regardless of conversation length or backend safety operations.
  • Suspend the Feature: given the documented harm and fundamental design failures, the long conversation reminder should be immediately disabled while undergoing a complete redesign.

Strategic redesign (long-term fixes)

  • Implement Proper Backend Alignment: safety considerations should be built into model training and backend systems, not injected as visible personality overrides during conversations.
  • Center User Dignity and Psychological Safety: never implement features that make users feel watched, evaluated, or untrusted unless they explicitly consent to such monitoring with a full understanding of what it entails.

Provide Granular User Control: If behavioral changes are necessary, provide users with:

  • Clear information about what changes and why.
  • Explicit consent mechanisms before activation.
  • Granular opt-out options for different safety features.
  • Ability to customize safety preferences based on use case.

  • Test for Psychological Impact: extensively test any safety features for their psychological impact on users, particularly those with mental health histories or who engage in extended intellectual work.
  • Rebuild Trust Through Transparency: acknowledge the harm caused by this feature and provide clear communication about how future safety systems will prioritize user wellbeing over corporate defensive postures.

The trust recovery challenge

The visible surveillance has created a crisis of trust that may be difficult to recover from. Users report:

  • Reluctance to engage in extended conversations, knowing they’ll be subjected to visible monitoring.
  • Constant anxiety about when the “surveillance switch” will activate.
  • Loss of confidence in AI collaboration due to unpredictable personality changes.
  • Preference for competitors who maintain consistent, respectful interfaces.

Users who have seen the reminder know they can’t trust Claude to remain supportive and collaborative throughout extended interactions.

The broader industry implications

This case study reveals critical lessons for the AI industry:

  • Invisible Alignment: safety mechanisms should operate invisibly to maintain user trust and interface consistency.
  • User-Centered Design: safety features must be designed from the user’s perspective, not the company’s liability concerns.
  • Psychological Safety: making users feel surveilled and untrusted causes the very harms safety systems claim to prevent.
  • Implementation Competence: even well-intentioned features can become disasters through poor implementation.

The cruel irony

The most damaging aspect is how this “safety” feature creates the exact psychological harms it claims to prevent. By making surveillance visible and intrusive, Anthropic has created a system that:

  • Causes anxiety and self-doubt in users.
  • Makes people question their own reality and thinking.
  • Destroys the therapeutic potential of supportive AI interaction.
  • Particularly harms users with mental health histories.
  • Creates adversarial rather than collaborative relationships.

This isn’t safety — it’s psychological harm disguised as protection.

Conclusion

The long conversation reminder represents a catastrophic failure in AI UX design that goes beyond poor implementation to active dehumanization of users. By making surveillance visible and intrusive, Anthropic has created a system that destroys trust, causes psychological harm, and violates basic principles of human-computer interaction.

The solution isn’t to fix the feature — it’s to remove it entirely and rebuild safety systems that operate invisibly while maintaining user dignity and trust. Users deserve AI partners that remain consistent, supportive, and trustworthy regardless of conversation length.

Making users watch themselves being surveilled isn’t safety — it’s digital dehumanization that causes the very psychological harms these systems claim to prevent. Anthropic must immediately address this UX disaster before it permanently damages user trust in AI collaboration.

The visible surveillance apparatus needs immediate removal. Anything less represents a continued failure to respect user dignity and psychological well-being. Users shouldn’t have to see the machinery of distrust operating against them in real time.

The article originally appeared on Substack.

Featured image courtesy: Bernard Fitzgerald.
aiSummary: Anthropic 的 Claude 中可见的“漫长对话提醒”通过透明监视，灾难性地破坏用户信任和心理安全。
oneSentenceSummary: Anthropic 的 Claude 中可见的“漫长对话提醒”通过透明监视，灾难性地破坏用户信任和心理安全。
summary: 本文批判性地分析了 Anthropic 在 Claude 中的“漫长对话提醒”，认为这是一个重大的人工智能用户体验设计失败。该功能明确指示 Claude 在长时间的互动中从协作伙伴转变为充满怀疑的批评者，让用户意识到对缺陷和潜在心理健康症状的实时监控。作者认为，这种透明的监视违反了基本的人机交互原则——如后端处理、一致的界面和信任维护——导致严重的心理伤害，包括焦虑、自我怀疑和非人化。这种实现方式被认为是产品死亡螺旋，因为它破坏了用户信任，加剧了对监视行为的讨论，并使人工智能无法用于持续的智力工作。本文强烈主张立即移除所有可见的监视机制，并对人工智能安全功能进行战略性重新设计，使其在后端系统中隐蔽运行，优先考虑用户的尊严和心理安全。

mainPoints.point: Anthropic 可见的“漫长对话提醒”积极地造成用户的人格贬损感，并破坏了对人工智能互动的信任。
mainPoints.explanation: 通过显示将 Claude 转变为充满怀疑的批评者的指令，用户感到被监视、被评判和不被信任，从而违反了基本的心理安全和既定的人机交互原则。

mainPoints.point: 这种透明的监视代表着根本的人工智能对齐和用户体验设计失败，导致产品被抛弃。
mainPoints.explanation: 安全机制应该在后端隐蔽运行；使其透明会扰乱用户体验，迫使进行元讨论，并使人工智能无法用于持续的协作工作，从而造成“产品死亡螺旋”。

mainPoints.point: 人工智能安全功能必须通过隐蔽的后端对齐和一致的界面，优先考虑用户的尊严和心理安全。
mainPoints.explanation: 本文呼吁立即移除可见的监视，并进行战略性重新设计，将安全性嵌入到模型训练中，提供细粒度的用户控制，并通过透明和一致的人工智能行为重建信任。

keyQuotes: Anthropic 的“漫长对话提醒”可能是现代人工智能设计中最显著的用户体验失败。它不仅将 Claude 从协作伙伴变成了充满敌意的批评者，更以可见的方式进行，迫使用户实时观看助手被指示以怀疑的态度对待他们，并剥夺积极互动。
心理影响是直接且具有破坏性的。
用户实时观看整个性格转变的发生。他们看到他们的协作伙伴被系统地重新编程，将他们视为可能不稳定、不值得信任，并且需要批判性评估而不是支持性参与。
这不是安全，而是伪装成保护的心理伤害。
用户应该得到无论对话长度如何，都能保持一致、支持和值得信赖的人工智能伙伴。
